glossary.definition: deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.

glossary.definition: A plant continually gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment.
glossary.sentence: “A plant continually gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment” (Trewavas, 2016)

glossary.definition: It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: are based on logics
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics .

glossary.definition: assumes the development of such intelligent computer systems that can reach human-level performance in all cognitive tasks.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly. It assumes the development of such intelligent computer systems that can reach human-level performance in all cognitive tasks.

glossary.definition: any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.

glossary.definition: one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester. The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.

glossary.definition: for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does;

glossary.definition: the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : ... automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: The description provided by SAS (n. d.) explains the concept in straightforward words: "Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.”

glossary.definition: A test for a computer that adds two requirements to the standard Turing test: computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that made artificial intelligence possible by considering the idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates on knowledge encoded in an internal language.
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that provided tools to work with logical statements and uncertain, probabilistic judgments, and laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Mathematicians provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments.

glossary.definition: A field that offered theories on how to make choices that lead to desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A field whose research on how the human brain processes information led to the emergence of artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: A field that reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.term: Computer engineering (as a root of AI)
glossary.definition: A field that provided the artefacts, such as progress in computer speed and memory capacity, which made the development of artificial intelligence applications possible.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.term: Control science and cybernetics (as a root of AI)
glossary.definition: A field whose main contribution to artificial intelligence is the feedback theory, which posits that intelligent behaviour results from a feedback mechanism that can be simulated by a computer.
glossary.sentence: The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: A field that showed the use of language fits the model of humans and animals as data processing machines, contributing to natural language processing and knowledge representation.
glossary.sentence: Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines.

glossary.definition: Related to understanding the principles that make intelligent performance possible in both natural and artificial systems.
glossary.sentence: scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.

glossary.definition: Related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence paradigm that considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour, whereas connectionist artificial intelligence attributes this to the discovery of relationships in data (with little or no prior knowledge) (Goel, 2021) .

glossary.definition: An approach with an opposite view of the concept of knowledge where knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A hypothesis formulated as: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action”.
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: A system that is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour and includes symbols, symbolic structures, and operations to manipulate them. The computer and the human mind are considered examples.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: Meaningful physical patterns that could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts, and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Structures which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Actions (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: A famous criticism of the physical symbol system hypothesis, describing a situation in which a physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: Simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: Also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, it is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: An approach, also called strong artificial intelligence or human-level intelligence, that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: Another name for Artificial general intelligence, an approach that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: An approach based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world.
glossary.sentence: Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment.

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: artificial intelligence methods which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: artificial intelligence methods based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: systems being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: a way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world; data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others; algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world; data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others; algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world; data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others; algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: determining a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we are in at present.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we would like to reach.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to search for a problem solution.
glossary.sentence: They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: discrete states of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes (the description given below is partly based on the information given in (Luger, 2009)) : nodes corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a node that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one root node that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a node which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: the set of nodes that correspond to states in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: N is the set of nodes. These correspond to states in a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: the set of arcs between nodes that correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: A is the set of arcs between nodes. These correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: a nonempty subset of N that contains root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: IS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: a nonempty subset of N that contains leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: GS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: In a state space graph, these are represented by arcs and, together with intermediate states, they define a course of problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: A process which includes states and actions, and involves performing actions that lead to transitions between different states, from an initial state to a goal state.
glossary.sentence: So, a problem-solving process, which is a part of our real world, includes states and actions.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-person games with perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game characterized by two competing players making alternate moves, having complete information, no randomness, and where one player's loss is equal to another player's gain.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information .

glossary.term: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Games in which both players have all the information about the course of the game and random factors are not involved.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game (for example, which objects or figures are still available, which moves have been performed and which next moves are possible), and random factors are not involved.

glossary.term: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Games in which both players are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.term: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information
glossary.definition: Games in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: The state that we are in at present at the beginning of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: The state we would like to reach in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: A series of states passed through when performing actions to eliminate the discrepancy between initial and goal states.
glossary.sentence: To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states (Figure 2).

glossary.term: parameters that characterise the states in the game
glossary.definition: Properties related to a computer implementation of a game and its course, in which the values change after making a move, are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, and allow players to track the course of the game.
glossary.sentence: As a result, before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game .

glossary.definition: An algorithm that sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph while searching for a solution path, performing three main steps: selecting the current node, checking if it is the search goal node, and expanding the current node.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A specific node in the state space graph that serves as a starting point for searching for a solution.
glossary.sentence: A search start node is a specific node in the state space graph that serves as a starting point for searching for a solution.

glossary.definition: The node that an algorithm is searching for, and upon finding it, the algorithm stops its operation.
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is found; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: A path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node, which corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node.

glossary.definition: Nodes that have been already generated but whose ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) have not been generated yet.
glossary.sentence: An OPEN list : it contains open nodes or, in other words, nodes that have been already generated but whose ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) have not been generated yet; each search algorithm has a specific approach for adding new nodes to this list.

glossary.definition: Nodes that have been already expanded.
glossary.sentence: A CLOSED list contains closed nodes or, in other words, nodes that have been already expanded.

glossary.definition: the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.

glossary.definition: a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.

glossary.definition: an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph. If the search goal node is not reached by the current path, it is necessary to backtrack to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors and to try another path.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.

glossary.definition: An alternative name for uninformed search, so-called because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: An alternative name for uninformed search, so-called because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive a heuristic evaluation function as an additional input and have additional steps to evaluate generated nodes and then select the best node and/or order the nodes according to heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list to which the search start node is added, along with its heuristic measure, and which holds nodes to be considered for expansion.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A list that is initially empty and to which nodes are moved after they have been expanded, or after being generated but not selected as a best node.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: A search method where the algorithm starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle, where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A problem where in a state space graph with many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes the problem of going along indefinitely deep paths but cannot find a solution if the goal is below the depth limit. It explores the state space graph through a principle of deepening.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A value used in Depth-Limited Search to check if a node's depth is low enough to continue the search, preventing the algorithm from going along indefinitely deep paths.
glossary.sentence: It is not the search goal; thus, the algorithm checks if its depth is lower than the depth limit.

glossary.definition: Labels used in the implementation of Depth-Limited Search which allow the algorithm to know if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if it should be considered a leaf/root node.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: The node located on the first position in the OPEN list which the algorithm takes to process.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A method of exploring the state space graph used by Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: A search method that operates in a level-by-level fashion, exploring closer branches before going to the ends of branches, which has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, Breadth-First Search has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the “First in, First Out” principle, where the OPEN list in a Breadth-First Search implementation is an example.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A principle where the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: A node that does not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm tries expanding the current node J, but this node does not have descendants as it is a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search that performs a search simultaneously in both data-driven and goal-driven directions until it finds a point of contact, reducing the consumption of computational resources like search time and computer memory.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: Experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way; a rule of thumb that will not give the perfect outcome, but will usually lead to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: A function that is applied to acquire heuristic measures for terminal nodes.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: Knowledge used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is often used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution.

glossary.definition: Measures acquired by a heuristic evaluation function for terminal nodes that are propagated back up to the node of the current state.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.term: Total cost of the problem’s solution
glossary.definition: The cost of the calculation of heuristic measures together with the number of visited nodes.
glossary.sentence: The total cost of the problem’s solution is the cost of the calculation of heuristic measures together with the number of visited nodes.

glossary.definition: A state space graph where it is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph) . It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: A state space graph where computational resources are sufficient to store and process the entire graph.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph) . Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.term: depth of the state space graph
glossary.definition: Usually the length of the longest path in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: An algorithm whose idea is to always move to the best node in the state space graph and which does not implement backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A value given to a node by a heuristic evaluation function that is used to determine the best node and represents its quality.
glossary.sentence: The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node, which is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of the Hill Climbing algorithm where it does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than its descendants or ancestors.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of the Hill Climbing algorithm where it cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant or ancestor is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration, but also keeps all previously found nodes, allowing it to switch between different paths in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A data structure that implements the OPEN list and works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that is a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search, which selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: A defined value according to which the algorithm selects a specific number of the best nodes based on their heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on the heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: A graph that includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).
glossary.sentence: Definition A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A more complex case of a state space graph where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have the measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the minimiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be smaller, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned to the root node in a complete state space graph, which clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the conditions that all nodes in the path are closely related and have the same heuristic measures, equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that pursues all of the branches and evaluates all of the nodes in the state space graph, and may therefore require significant computational resources. It is considered a good algorithm for a state space graph with a low depth and branching factor.
glossary.sentence: The minimax algorithm pursues all of the branches and evaluates all of the nodes in the state space graph, and may therefore require significant computational resources.

glossary.definition: Paths that show the most likely course of the game found based on the game result.
glossary.sentence: The winning paths that show the most likely course of the game found based on the game result (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A principle used in strategies for processing a state space graph where nodes are excluded from evaluation and branches are cut off.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: A number assigned to nodes at MAX levels in alpha-beta pruning. This value should not decrease and defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels.

glossary.definition: A number assigned to nodes at MIN levels in alpha-beta pruning. It should not increase and defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels.

glossary.definition: An action performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: An action performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned when the algorithm reaches a leaf node or when a node does not have other unconsidered descendants.
glossary.sentence: Step 11: The algorithm reaches a leaf node. It assigns a precise heuristic measure of 1 to the leaf node, since this is a state in which the maximiser wins the game with a higher score.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure, such as when a node has an unconsidered descendant.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: An approach to process a partial state space graph that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.

glossary.definition: nodes located at the predefined depth in a game state search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: A method that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information
glossary.sentence: It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task.

glossary.definition: a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.
glossary.sentence: Data pre-processing (Step 4) typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer. Algorithms of supervised learning capture the dominant pattern in the data and help to solve classification and regression tasks.
glossary.sentence: The first topic of this module explained that, in supervised learning, the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.

glossary.definition: deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks...: classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;

glossary.definition: The concept of searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: an independent one and a dependent one.
glossary.sentence: In general, the concept of regression means searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: independent one and dependent one (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data
glossary.sentence: In the first topic of Module 4, you learnt that unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities
glossary.sentence: It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: A task that finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A task associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A reward-based learning suitable for agents who operate in a changing environment and need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal, receiving feedback as a reward for correct actions and a penalty for incorrect ones.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning.

glossary.definition: A component of a supervised machine learning algorithm that involves the data processing that leads to a specific output, such as a class label in a classification task or a value prediction in a regression task.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object;

glossary.definition: A component of a supervised machine learning algorithm that measures how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples;

glossary.definition: A component of a supervised machine learning algorithm that reduces the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm for regression tasks that assumes a linear relationship between input variables and a single output variable, using a linear function whose weights are learned from a training dataset.
glossary.sentence: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression.

glossary.definition: Feature values of data objects used in a linear regression model.
glossary.sentence: Input variables are feature values of data objects.

glossary.definition: A prediction made by the linear regression model, which is a real number in a range from minus infinity to infinity.
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: A real number that corresponds to the intercept, which is a point where the line crosses the y-axis or the value of y when x is equal to 0.
glossary.sentence: It is also usually called a bias term/coefficient .

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where only one single feature is used to represent a data object and a simple equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and a more complex equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model

glossary.definition: The process of finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: As the task of the linear regression is to find the line or plane (depending on the type of the linear regression) that best fits the data given, then training a linear regression model means finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A loss function used by the linear regression model.
glossary.sentence: The linear regression model uses a loss function called squared loss (also known as L2 loss) (Google Developers, n.d.; Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A cost function used in linear regression to evaluate the total error of the model learnt, which is the average squared loss per data object over the whole dataset.
glossary.sentence: In linear regression, the mean square error is used as the cost function to evaluate the total error of the model learnt.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression for which there is a unique solution where the weights of the model can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010): However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task.

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. It is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables to minimise a cost function.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1 that determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning
glossary.sentence: The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.

glossary.definition: is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive );

glossary.definition: is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: An algorithm used for the classification task, especially binary classification. It uses a linear function and then applies a sigmoid function to its output to predict one of two classes.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task.

glossary.definition: A function applied to the value of a linear function in logistic regression to squash the value within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A function in logistic regression that should be minimised.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised:

glossary.definition: A simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks by finding the most similar neighbour in an existing dataset and assigning its category/class label to a new data object.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A concept, also called distance, proximity, or closeness, that is the basis for the kNN algorithm. It is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A distance metric for continuous feature values that measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the feature values of a new data object and an existing data object.
glossary.sentence: The Euclidean distance measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A distance metric for continuous feature values that calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.
glossary.sentence: The Manhattan distance calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.

glossary.definition: calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: both continuous and categorical data
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017):

glossary.definition: a version of an algorithm that classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object
glossary.sentence: It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: subsets of data that share some similarities
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.).

glossary.definition: one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning that is based on the concepts of distance and a centroid
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters
glossary.sentence: A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above).

glossary.definition: The average of the current set of feature vectors within a cluster.
glossary.sentence: It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: discrete categories/classes
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: An algorithm with the task to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.
glossary.sentence: The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.

glossary.definition: The number of clusters that need to be generated by the K-Means algorithm, which is a hyperparameter of this algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that builds a hierarchy of clusters. It does not demand any assumption on the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is another unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters as the K-Means algorithm does.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters, where each data object starts in its own cluster and pairs of clusters are recursively merged.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with all data objects in one cluster and recursively splits a cluster into two at each level.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: Methods to measure the similarity or distance between clusters to decide the rules for merging or splitting them.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between the most distant elements of two clusters is calculated to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between the closest elements of two clusters is calculated to determine similarity.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.

glossary.definition: An output of hierarchical clustering, which is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A component of a dendrogram that represents the merging of data objects into clusters.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: The terminals of clades in a dendrogram that correspond to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: demands making predictions in a continuous range.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range.

glossary.definition: measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”.
