glossary.definition: is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.

glossary.definition: a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.
glossary.sentence: Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.definition: (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric for multiclass classification calculated by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: An instance where a condition is present but not detected, such as a patient suffering from an illness but not being diagnosed as such.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example of a medical diagnosis, in which a patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such represents a false negative, something the doctor wants to avoid—which means that recall should be small.

glossary.definition: The inverse relationship between precision and recall, where increasing one metric reduces the other.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: A metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall, yielding a high score only if both precision and recall are high.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A visualization of a classifier's behavior under different parameter values, which plots the false positive rate (x-axis) versus the true positive rate (y-axis).
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A single-number summary of the ROC curve that measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath it, used for comparing different classifiers.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) .

glossary.definition: A metric for evaluating clustering performance that does not require ground-truth labels and represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in neighboring clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score .

glossary.definition: The average distance between each point within a single cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: The average distance between all clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: A plot that represents the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A concept that is closely related to artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: The cells that the human brain consists of.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions from a neuron's cell body that look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension from a neuron's cell body along which a sending neuron transmits a signal.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: Synapses that promote the firing of a neuron and can be considered to have a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: Synapses that inhibit the firing of a neuron and can be considered to have a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location.

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron, defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943, which has one or more inputs, a set of real-valued weights, an output, a function for combining input signals, a transfer (activation) function, and sometimes a bias input.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons, sometimes also called input signals. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: A set of real-valued values, one for each input, that express the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. They are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks and are determined in the process of neural network learning.
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: A numerical value that can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value, which is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value.

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships;

glossary.definition: An input that provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin, allowing the learning algorithm to tune its weight.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: Activation functions, also called hard limit functions, that are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A system that comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern, typically arranged in a hierarchy of layers.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Single neurons within an artificial neural network, also called nodes.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: The layers formed by neurons connected to the external environment.
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers.

glossary.definition: The layers of neurons situated between the input and output layers.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that has connections only in one direction, where every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' units and delivers output to 'downstream' units, with no loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its own inputs, meaning its response to a given input depends on its initial state, which may depend on previous inputs.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: The process of modifying weights in a particular way, where a learning rule is applied to modify the weights if there is a difference between the desired output and the network's output.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: The property of artificial neural networks to be resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections, with network operation changing very little because knowledge is distributed throughout the network. Also known as graceful degradation.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: identifying the structure or pattern in data
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations.
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: finding the "best" organization of constraints
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables
glossary.sentence: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: The simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions.

glossary.definition: accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons
glossary.sentence: It has an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers.

glossary.definition: sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: The propagation of inputs over the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding.

glossary.definition: The propagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.

glossary.definition: to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Anyone if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts, and relations are represented using a referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8).

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: An essential feature of frame-based expert systems where sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation method that stores knowledge about each object in a structured way, provides hierarchical organization, allows representation of various knowledge, supports inheritance, and allows adding executable demons to slots. They are more suitable for representing static, not dynamic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation; a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts within a frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represent extended knowledge about slot values, tell the inference engine how to process the property, allow setting a slot value, and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames linked together.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” .

glossary.definition: A type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Classes derived from other classes that can contain new slots not included in the class frames but which characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5).

glossary.definition: A frame that represents detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.
glossary.sentence: It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A principle used to relate two frames, meaning that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames.

glossary.definition: A relation defined between instance frames and their class frames, and between sub-class frames and their parent class frames, represented as a Class slot.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: A relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts, where frames for components are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: A relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A situation where a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: The ability to add regular procedures and/or demons to a slot.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base and is triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Frames that are acquired when demons are assigned to them.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A standard demon that is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate or set it.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A standard demon that is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A standard demon that is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A standard demon that is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A demon that will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: An engine that acquires knowledge about an object/concept by finding a corresponding frame and checking its slots. If knowledge is not found, it can perform inheritance, check inherited demons, and explore local relations with other frames until the value of a specified property is obtained.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.term: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based systems that represent a set of problem-solving instructions rather than knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems.

glossary.definition: The simplest type of rule, which is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem, presented in the format: IF condition THEN consequence.
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule .

glossary.definition: An intelligent system where statements are added to the THEN part of its rules.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: An intelligent system where the THEN part of its rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts: a knowledge base with production rules, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component of a rule-based expert system that stores facts acquired from the user, conclusions from the reasoning process, and other case-specific data, thereby keeping track of the current state of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The rule-based expert system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process, which is contained in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: An essential component for any expert system that should explain its reasoning and justify its output.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: An additional component of an expert system intended to edit rules in the knowledge base and to check the system's performance after updates.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The basis of the inference mechanism that implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by matching the content of the working memory with production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism.

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
